hook: You already know that in a right triangle, if you have two sides, you can find the third using the Pythagorean theorem. But what if you only know one side and one angle — and you need to find the missing sides? For example, a ramp rises at a 20° angle and the horizontal distance is 15 feet: how tall is the ramp? You can't use the Pythagorean theorem alone here — so what tool will let you solve this?
concept: Solving a right triangle means finding all three sides and all three angles. When you know one acute angle and one side, you can use the trig ratios — sine, cosine, and tangent — to set up an equation and solve for any unknown side. Formally, if $\theta$ is a known acute angle, then $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, and $\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$.
anatomy: Every solved right triangle problem has the same key components: the reference angle $\theta$ (the known acute angle you build your ratios from), the three sides labeled relative to that angle — the opposite side (across from $\theta$), the adjacent side (next to $\theta$, not the hypotenuse), and the hypotenuse (always opposite the right angle and always the longest side). The trig ratio you choose depends entirely on which two of these three roles are involved: the known side and the unknown side.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Given: right triangle with } \theta = 32°, \text{ hypotenuse } = 10. \text{ Find the opposite side } x.
worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying which sides are involved relative to our reference angle of 32°. We know the hypotenuse (10) and want the opposite side ($x$), so the ratio that connects opposite and hypotenuse is sine.
We write the sine ratio with our known angle: $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, plugging in 32° and 10. This equation now has only one unknown, $x$, which we can isolate.
We multiply both sides by 10 to isolate $x$. Multiplying by the denominator is always the move when the unknown is in the numerator of a trig equation.
We evaluate $\sin(32°) \approx 0.5299$ using a calculator (make sure it's in degree mode!), then multiply to get $x \approx 5.30$. The opposite side is approximately 5.30 units long.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Given: right triangle with } \theta = 47°, \text{ adjacent side } = 8. \text{ Find the opposite side } x.
partial_example.description_latex: Start by figuring out which trig ratio connects the adjacent side (which we know) and the opposite side (which we want). The ratio that uses opposite over adjacent is tangent, so we will set up a tangent equation.
We've set up the equation correctly: $\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$ with $\theta = 47°$ and adjacent $= 8$. Now isolate $x$ by multiplying both sides by 8.
Multiply both sides by 8 and then evaluate $\tan(47°)$ on your calculator to find the value of $x$. Write your final answer rounded to two decimal places.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{In a right triangle, } \theta = 45° \text{ and the hypotenuse is } 10. \text{ Find the opposite side.}
\text{In a right triangle, } \theta = 58° \text{ and the adjacent side is } 6. \text{ Find the hypotenuse.}
\text{In a right triangle, } \theta = 33° \text{ and the opposite side is } 9. \text{ Find the adjacent side.}
\text{In a right triangle, one acute angle is } 22° \text{ and the hypotenuse is } 14. \text{ Find both legs and the other acute angle.}
\text{A ladder leans against a wall at a } 70° \text{ angle with the ground. The ladder is } 12 \text{ ft long. How high up the wall does it reach?}

common_mistakes: Students sometimes assign opposite and adjacent relative to the right angle instead of the reference angle $\theta$. The labels opposite and adjacent always depend on which acute angle you choose as $\theta$ — fix this by circling $\theta$ first and then labeling the sides from its perspective.

When the unknown is in the denominator (e.g., $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{h}$), students solve by multiplying by $\cos\theta$ instead of dividing, getting $h = \text{adjacent} \cdot \cos\theta$ instead of $h = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\cos\theta}$. Remember: if the unknown is the denominator, divide the known side by the trig value.

Students evaluate trig functions with their calculator in radian mode instead of degree mode, getting wildly wrong decimal values. Always check that your calculator displays 'DEG' before computing, since $\sin(30°) = 0.5$ but $\sin(30 \text{ rad}) \approx -0.988$.
untested_variants: Finding a missing angle using inverse trig functions ($\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$, $\tan^{-1}$) when two sides are given but no angle is known.
Solving a right triangle embedded in a larger geometric figure, such as finding the diagonal of a rectangle or the height of an isosceles triangle by splitting it.
Problems involving two right triangles sharing a side, requiring the student to solve one triangle first and use that result as input to solve the second.
Angle of elevation and angle of depression word problems where the student must first draw and label the diagram before setting up any trig equation.
